origin_explanation: from Heb. raˀem, also Amh. raˀem (from Geez). In the other Semitic languages: Ar. rim, rim 'white antelope', SAr. bqr rymn (W. Müller), Aram. raˀema, reˀma 'wild bull', Syr. raymā, Ug. rˀm-m (de Moor 199), Akk. rimu 'wild bull'. For more details on Ar. and G. raˀem, see Hommel 258, 367.

meaning: tal (of column)', (Lt) title below the king (Amh. ras). raˀus 'constituted chief, uppermost, foremost, chief, commander, (Gr) that is the highest'; raˀsannā 'princely dignity, office of chief, leader- ship, charge (of an overseer)'; tarˀās (tarˀas) 'head of the bed, head support (bolster, pillow), what lies at the head, what is near one's head' (cp. also rgd, tǝrgād); with suffix pronouns: tarˀāsihu 'at his head'; tarˀāsa 'at the head'; tatarˀasa, see trˀs; matarˀas, see trˀs;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. raˀs 'head', SAr. rˀs, 'head', reš 'head', Soq. rey, re, Heb. rō(ˀ)š, rīš, Pf. rōˀš, Md. Ug. rīš, rīš, Ph. rˀš, Akk. rēšu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. raˀsi 'head', Te. rāˀas 'head', Amh. ras 'head' (with suffix pronouns 'self'), ˀərsu (from ˀərs-u) 'he' (lit. 'his head'), Har. urūs 'head'. Gur. rās 'self'. See also rāyas.

meaning: (Dillmann 1401) 'corals' (?), (KG) = Amh. biwalo gǝmga 'silk cloth the color of chickpea flowers';
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. rā(ˀ)mōtḥ through Gk. ramōth.

meaning: become (be) chief, rise above' (denominative); ˀarˀasa 'make (someone) chief'; tarˀasa 'be made chief, be placed (set up) at the head, be at the head of'; raˀ(a)s (pl. ˀar(a)ˀast, pl. of pl. ˀar(a)ˀastāt) 'head, top, summit, chief (thing), chief (leader), beginning, capital (of column), principal (mon- ey), number, sum, chapter, title (of a book)'; with suffix pronouns 'self'; e.g. raˀasu 'he him- self', ˀammar raˀasǝya 'I know myself'; also 'own' with a noun in a construct state + raˀas- with suffix pronouns; e.g. beta raˀasu 'his own house'; lallarˀasu 'each'; e.g. lasabˀa ˀalam lallarˀasu 'to each person of the world'; (raˀasa mangǝśt 'capital city, head of state'); (raˀasa kǝhnāt 'archpriest'); (raˀasa diyāqonāt 'archdeacon'); (raˀasa nǝwāy 'principal of money'); (raˀasa mǝrazant 'cornerstone'); ˀar(a)ˀast 'index'; (ˀar(a)ˀasta nagar 'topics, table of contents'); rāˀas (pl. rāˀasān) 'head, chief, principal, capi-
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. raˀs 'head', SAr. rˀs, 'head', reš 'head', Soq. rey, re, Heb. rō(ˀ)š, rīš, Pf. rōˀš, Md. Ug. rīš, rīš, Ph. rˀš, Akk. rēšu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. raˀsi 'head', Te. rāˀas 'head', Amh. ras 'head' (with suffix pronouns 'self'), ˀərsu (from ˀərs-u) 'he' (lit. 'his head'), Har. urūs 'head'. Gur. rās 'self'. See also rāyas.

meaning: (T.Sw. in D 1401), the name of (God) the Father' (seems to be a magical name).

manifest (tr.), become visible, show oneself, reveal oneself, be manifested, be seen, be made visible, become conspicuous, seem, show'; (zayyāstarˀi 'invisible'); (zayyāstarˀi 'invisible'); (zayyāstarˀi 'invisible'); (Gr.452) 'compare (persons or things)';
(pl. raˀayt), act. part. of raˀaya 'seer, observer, diviner, (Gr.154) prophet'; raˀaye hǝbwāt 'soothsayer, seer of hidden things'; (raˀaye kokab 'astrologer');
'vision, gaze, face, image, likeness, counte- nance, form, aspect, sight, revelation, appear- ance, apocalypse';
'appearance, appearing (n.), vision, shape, image, countenance, looks, sight, spectacle';
'exemplar, similitude, likeness, example, appearance, spectacle, figure, form, shape, type, image, aspect, norm, pattern, mod- el, standard, copy, transcription; just as, like, as';
in baˀalā ˀastarˀayo 'Feast of the Transfiguration';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. raˀā (rˀy) 'see', SAr. ry, Heb. raˀā (rˀy), Aram. *reˀw 'appearance', Syr. rǝhā 'observe' (Brockelmann 1928:715), Ug. ru 'appearance' (de Moor 74). On the Aramaic particle hārē, ˀārē, see Barth 1911:27-37, Landberg 1923:1315ff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāˀayā 'see', Te. rāˀa, Har. riˀa, Gur. ˀarˀa. Amh. raˀay 'vision' (from Geez).

meaning: causative; 'who (that) quakes, who trembles, seized with trembling';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (rḍd) ˀirtaˀada 'tremble', Heb. rāˀad, Aram. rǝˀad, Syr. (ˀe)rˀaˀad, Akk. rādu (connected by von Soden 941 with the root rwd, below). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāˀadā 'tremble', Te. rāˀada, Amh. radā, Gur. (a)raˀadā. Har. raˀdi 'thunder', Gur. rǝğa 'malaria fever' (something that causes shaking).
SEMITIC: Heb. rāˀam 'thunder' (v), Aram.-Syr. rǝˀem 'thunder, resound', Md. rīm, rum; related to Akk. ramāmu 'roar'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. rämta (from räm-ta) 'tramp of marching feet'.

meaning: (yǝrˀaw) (K*) 'yoke, join' (de- nominative from ˀarˀut 'yoke', or reconstructed by K from ˀarˀut);

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to ry, as in Heb. reˀa 'friend, companion', Aram. rˀ. Akk. rāˀu, Ug. rˀ. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāˀwe (with metathesis) 'have inter- course', Te. rāˀawa. See also mrw, above.

meaning: (yǝrˀay) (K*) 'herd, tend (animals or people), feed (a flock), graze (tr., intr), pasture (tr., intr), take care of cattle, shepherd';
'graze together, feed in common, (Gr) graze in turns';
'cattle, flock, herd, pasture, sheepfold'; mar'ay (pl. mar'ay), same; mar'ay 'pasture'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ra' (ry) 'graze, tend', SAr. ry, Soq. rew, Heb. ra' (ry), Aram.-Syr. ra'd, Md. raa, Ph. r, Ug. ry 'herdsman', Akk. rew 'graze'. Barth 1902:46-7 derives the meaning 'herd, tend' from a more general meaning of 'ry 'be mindful, be regardful' (Ar. ra' 'show regard for') which he connects with Syr. reyana (from ry) 'mind, conscience', Aram. rayona 'thought', as against Schulthess 70 and Brockelmann 1928:738. ETHIOPIC: Te. ra'a 'pasture, tend (cattle)', mar'ay 'pasture ground'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. ra'ayi 'giants' (from Geez).
see rababa.
from Heb. rabbi, through Gk. rhabbi; also Amh. rabbi 'teacher', Tna. rabbi 'master', Te. rabbi 'God'. See also rabbawa, rapi, rabboni.
denominative

meaning: 'fourth'; raba'ta 'd 'fourth part'; (raba'ta 'for the fourth time'); rabba'i 'who believes in four persons of the Godhead'; rabba'e 'fourfold, act of making four'; raba't 'fourth part, a fourth'; raba't 'square' (n.); raba'awi (fem. raba'awit), raba'ay 'fourth'; raba'it 'fourth part of a day'; maraba' 'one who believes in four persons of the Godhead, quadrinitarian'; maraba't 'square (n.), squareness, four sides, (Gr.152) fourth floor'; tarba't (Lt) 'fourth floor'; tarba't 'fourfold, quadruple, four times, four sides of a square, the number four, the four persons of Godhead'; tarbu'at (Lt) 'the four elements';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. arba' 'four', rabi' 'fourth', 'our 'four', rb 'one-fourth', Soq. 'erba'ah 'four', Heb.Aram.-Syr. arba' 'four', Heb. rba' 'fourth part', Md. arba' 'four', Ph. rb 'four', Ug. arb(t) 'four', rb 'fourth', Akk. arba'u 'four', rebu' 'fourth'. 'Forty' and 'Wednesday' are likewise formed from this root. ETHIOPIC: Te. arba' 'four', Tna. arba'at 'four', Id. arg. arbit, Gur. arbat, Amh. arat (with b becoming zero). 'Forty' and 'Wednesday' are likewise formed from this root.
From Ar. rabi'.

meaning: 'stretch, stretch out, expand, extend, spread out, scatter, (T.Y.M) hover' (that is, 'fly with spread wings'); rababa marbahta (or masgarta) 'cast a net'; rababa (Gr.151), causative; tarabba, pass., intr. of rababa; rabab 'stretch out from all sides'; rababi, act. part. of rababa; rabab, pass. part. of rababa; also 'unrolled, hovering'; rabba't 'expansion, extension';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. rabba 'place where one stretches out', marbabt, marbaht (pl. marabht) 'net, fisherman's net, trap, snare, (Gr.151) fishhook' (marbabta salomon 'Solomon's Net', a book containing incantations for conjuring up demons); see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:246. SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. rabba 'arrange' (Armbruster 121). It is not to be connected with Sem. rbb 'numerous' (Ar. rabba, Heb. rab) as suggested by Buhl 740. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rababa 'plait, catch fish with a net', rababa hala 'fly with spread wings', Te. rababa 'stretched out', Amh. rabbaba 'extend, spread, scatter, hover', rabbarraba 'strew, scatter about'. G. marbabt 'net, snare', cp. also G. marab, Marab, Amh. marab, marbab. Geez marbab could come from the meaning rbb 'extend, spread out' but could also be identified with Te. rabba (rbb) 'plait', Tna. rababa. For marbabt, see M. Cohen, Mélanges Massignon, vol. 1 (1956).327.
reconstructed from Heb. rab (rbb) 'be numerous'.
Amh. naffara 'boil (water)'.
From Ar. rububiyya 'lordship, mastership'.

meaning: 'gain, profit, benefit, make money, draw profit, be profitable, be useful, (K) augment, multiply'; rabaha 'exact interest'; rabaha 'make profitable, make someone profit (earn, gain, win), lend money in usury'; tarabha 'be gained, be profitable, have the advantage of gain, receive usury'; tarabaha 'exact interest from someone, gain, gain mutually, obtain profit, make profit through trade, (Gr) gain in an illicit manner'; astarabaha, causative of tarabha; rabahi, act. part. of rabha; also 'profitable, gainful';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. rabiha 'gain, profit' (is not a Geez loanword as suggested by Jeffery 138). SAr. rbh 'profit' (n.), Sh. erbah 'gain a profit'. Barth 1893:29 identifies it with the root rwh, as in PbH. rewah 'profit', Aram. ('ajrwah 'make profit, gain'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rabha 'gain, profit', Te. rabha 'be useful', Amh. rabba 'prosper, be useful', Gur. raba 'be better'. Note that Amh. rabba is not to be connected with Ug. rb 'great, chief', as suggested by Fisher 183. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. rabeh 'profit', Sa. rabah.
translation of the Hebrew proper name ra'uben.

meaning: 'master, teacher, leader, elder, senior, (Lt) ship owner, captain of a ship'; rabbanay (Lt) 'ship owner, captain of a ship'; rabbana (T.Y.M) 'teach' (denominative);
origin_explanation: from Aram. rabban 'teacher, master' through Ar. rabban 'master, captain of a ship'; also Soq. rehebon 'commander', Te. rabban 'helmsman (of a ship), captain'. For Ar. rabban, see Fraenkel 221-2, Landberg 1923:1059ff.
From Heb. ribboni, PbH. rabbani through Gk. rhabboni; also Ar. rabbaniyy 'doctor of law' (for which see Landberg 1923:1059, Jeffery 137-8).
see rbb I.

Gur. rādda. On Har. arda in ardawīǧ 'school' con-nected with G. rad 'disciple'; see Cerulli 1936:235. The root passed into Cushitic: Aw. ārdātū-x-a 'help', Som. arday 'student'. For G. ardā'a 'inform, report', cp. Tna. 'ardā'e 'announce the death of a relative', Har. ardā'a, Amh.Arg. arraddā. Gur. arraddā. It is generally identified with the root rd- 'help', but the connection is not clear unless being informed of the death of a relative is the equivalent of being helped. On the other hand, since G. 'ardā'a also means 'report, inform', the informing may easi-ly be referred to the informing of the death of a relative and there is no need to identify it with the meaning 'help'.
from Ar. ribawāt, pl. of ribwa 'myriad'; possibly also from Heb. rəḇāḇōṯ 'myriads'.
ETHIOPIC: To be identified with Tna. rawya 'vulture', Te. rawya 'stork', with b becoming w.
from Gk. rhoda (nom.-acc. pl. of rhodon 'rose'); also in Te.Tna.Amh. rādā, Syr. rodā; in Cushitic: Bil. rādā.

SEMITIC: Akk. redû, Ass. radā'u 'pursue'. ETHIOPIC: Te. rādā'a 'recapture (stolen herds), under-take a raid to recapture'; also in Cushitic: Som. rad-o 'pursuit of an enemy'. The root seems to be related to (rwd), roda, below.
SEMITIC: Ar. radda (rdd) 'reject, repel' (Ambros).
from Gk. rhedōn, gen. pl. of rheda 'chariot', of Latin (reda) origin.
From Gk. sardonyks. See sardonaks.

phonetics: (D 1401), rodās (K), the fifth nail with which Jesus was crucified;
meaning: the fifth nail with which Jesus was crucified;
origin_explanation: from Latin ROTAS (acc. pl. of rota 'wheel, wheel of torture'); see Euringer, ZS 6(1908).303-5, Streleyn 1955:80, n. 1, Worrell III 88, n. 7, and sādor below.
see warada.
see radā.
see rwd, roda.

'ardaya (Gr), causative. taradāya 'practice usury, lend at interest, receive interest'. SEMITIC: Ar. radā (rdy) 'increase, exceed', SAr. rdy 'interest' (W. Müller 1983:282), Soq. radā 'crease'. Note that Akk. reddû 'add' is considered by von Soden 967 a D-stem of redû 'accompany'. For a bibliography on this root, see Conti 1978:32. ETHIOPIC: Amh. rāde 'usury' (from Geez).

Note that H 213 wrongly renders Amh. sāffa (translation of raf'a) by 'be wide' instead of 'sew' (see also rafi, below). 'arfā'a (K*), causative. tarafa'a, passive of raf'a. rafā'i 'tailor, one who heals'. rafw, pass. part. of raf'a. rafi (T.M) 'one who sews, one who helps'. Note that M 138 wrongly renders Amh. sāfi (translation of G. rafi) given by Tayyā'a 'large, wide', whereas it means 'one who sews in this context'. raf'i (K.M) 'needlework'. raf'o 'tailor's work, sewn work, needlework'. raf'at 'seam, sewing'. marfā, pl. marfā'a, marfā'āt, (T) marfā 'needle'. marfā'a 'needle'. SEMITIC: Ar. rafā'a 'sew', SAr. rf 'safeguard, pro-tect', Heb. rāpā (rp') 'cure', Aram. rp' (Jean-Tijzer 282), Syr. rapā, Ph. rp', Ug. rp' (Caquot 402), Akk. rapā'u 'heal'. The connection with Soq. rof 'be healed' suggested by Brockelmann 1928:740 is not convincing. For the relationship between 'sew' and 'heal', cp. Ar. šafā (šfy) 'heal' and 'išfā 'awl' (Fraenkel 1898:68). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāf'e 'sew', Te. rāf'a 'tna. mārfa 'needle', Amh. mārfä, Gur. mārfä, Har. mārfī. A wārfa (with the instrumental m becoming w in root containing a labial f; see Cohen 1931:389). The root passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. raf 'sew', Qua. merfā 'needle', Som. marfi.

'arfādā (K*) 'do (something) late in the morn-ing'. radfādita gize (Lt) 'morning, forenoon'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'arfādā 'be late in the morning (in doing something)', Amh.Gaf. arfādā, Arg. arfādā. Bil. arfādā 'be late', Kam. raffāggo.
'arfādā (Lt) 'prepare abundantly'. SEMITIC: Ar. rafada 'put a saddle-pad on (a beast)', Heb. rāpad 'spread out'.
probably misreading of ራቅሐ and ራቅሐ raqha, raqha of the manuscript.

phonetics: (yərfāq) 'recline (at the table), lie down, sit (at the table to eat)'.
meaning: 'recline (at the table), lie down, sit (at the table to eat)'.
origin_explanation: 'arfāqa, causative. rafāqi, act. part. of rafaqa. marfāq, (D.T) marfāq 'lintel, doorstep, thresh-old'. marfāq, marfāq, (T) marfāq 'place where one reclines or sits (seat, bed), dining hall in a convent, banquet, feast, meal'. (rə'asa marfāq 'seat at the head of a table'). (liqa marfāq 'steward of a feast'). SEMITIC: Ar. (rfq), irtafaqa 'lean upon', mirfāq 'elbow' (lit. 'supporter'), Mh. refeqāt 'clothes peg' (lit. 'that which supports'), Heb. (rpq), hitrappeQ 'lean, support oneself', PBh. marpeq 'elbow', Aram. marpaqā. ETHIOPIC: Amh. rāffāqā 'sit at the table, sit down in council'.
from Ar. rafaqa 'be in company', rafīq 'companion'; also in Te. rāfāqā 'be a companion', Amh. rāfāqa 'comrade, friend'.

phonetics: (yərgā) 'curdle (into), coagulate (into), be congealed, be frozen solid, become thick, (Y) be quiet'.
meaning: 'curdle (into), coagulate (into), be congealed, be frozen solid, become thick, (Y) be quiet'.

phonetics: `arga` (curdle (tr), congeal (tr), condense'; `rag`a`i (KG), act. part. of `rag`a; `rag`a` 'congealed, coagulated, solid, firm, thick'; `rag`a`t (n) 'congealing, coagulating'; `arg`a`a` (Gr.452), part. of `arg`a; `marg`a` (Lt) 'curds (of milk), something solid-ified (ice, snow)'; `marg`a`a` dam [Lt] 'that makes the blood congeal')
meaning: `curdle (tr), congeal (tr), condense'; `rag`a`i (KG), act. part. of `rag`a; `rag`a` 'congealed, coagulated, solid, firm, thick'; `rag`a`t (n) 'congealing, coagulating'; `arg`a`a` (Gr.452), part. of `arg`a; `marg`a` (Lt) 'curds (of milk), something solid-ified (ice, snow)'; `marg`a`a` dam [Lt] 'that makes the blood congeal'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. rāgā` 'form a crust, come to rest'. The identification with Ar. rağā`a 'zurückkehren in die frühere Stelle oder Zustand' suggested by Barth 1893:8 (so also Palache 69) is unlikely. Note that Heb. rg` 'stir' is to be separated from rg` 'come to rest' (so also Noeldeke 1910:96, Buhl 745, as against Koehler 874 who derives these two meanings from a single root). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāg`e, rāg`e 'coagulate, be peaceful', Te. rāg`a, Har. rāg`a-a, Amh.Gur. rağğa; passed into Cushitic: Qem. rāg`a. Note the figurative meaning 'be calm, quiet' which occurs in Ethiopic and Hebrew.

(M 137 quotes Y and renders G. ragaba by 'be tender, be clement'; however Y translates G. ragaba with Amh. fārra b.b. 'fear', and M misread rarra b.b. 'be clement' for fārra b.b.); SEMITIC: Ar. rağaba 'fear'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tā)rāğābāğğābā 'tremble with fear'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. rāğğābā 'become slack, become loose (rope), become soft, be yielding (substance)'.

SEMITIC: Dillmann 316 connects it with Ar. rağiba 'be frightened'; rağb would then designate a fearful bird. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rağbī 'dove', Te. argab, Amh. argab, rağab; passed into Cushitic: Qem. ergeb, Qua. regeb.

phonetics: `rgd`, `argada` (MK) 'perform a special kind of ritual or funeral dance'; `taragda`, passive; `maragd`a 'a special kind of ritual or funeral dance'
meaning: `perform a special kind of ritual or funeral dance`; `taragda`, passive; `maragd`a 'a special kind of ritual or funeral dance'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğādā 'dance at funeral ceremo-nies', Amh. rāğğādā 'dance, mourn by beating the breast or by stamping the feet'. The root is related to Semitic rqd 'dance'. See also mrgd.

phonetics: `ragada` (yargad) (T), `ragasa` (T) 'kick, trample, stamp, strike convulsively with the feet'
meaning: `kick, trample, stamp, strike convulsively with the feet`
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. rakada 'stamp', Mh. rikōd 'kick'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğāšā 'kick, stamp', Te. rāğšā, Amh.Gur. rāğğātā, Har. rāğātā, Gaf. rāğātā. Note that Praetorius 1879:263 identifies it with the root rgt 'be sure'. Tna. rāğāšā, Amh. (tā)rāğāšā. Amh.Arg. arğat which would then mean 'einge-stampft > fest, sicher'. The root is related to ic: Sa.Af. rağād 'kick'.

phonetics: `ragafa I` (yargaf) (Y.M) 'embroider, paint (on a garment), decorate, adorn, make inci-sions, write'
meaning: `embroider, paint (on a garment), decorate, adorn, make inci-sions, write'
origin_explanation: raguf (T)rag`af 'decorated, variegated, embroi-dered, checkered structure (Ez. 40:17, transla-tion of Heb. rispā 'pavement, floor of a hall'). (T.OT) covered with fresh grass or rushes'. marğaf (K*) 'embroidered garment'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğğāfā 'embroider, weave'. Amh. rāğğāfā 'embroider a šämma-garment' (from Geez).

argafa (Lt) 'unsaddle'; raguf (K*) 'that which falls down (fruit, leaves)'. SEMITIC: Ar. rağafa 'agitate', Aram. rağap 'stir', Md. rgp 'flicker'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğāfā 'fall off (fruit, leaves)', Amh.Gur. rāğğāfā, Amh. also (tā)rāğğāfā, Har. rāğāfa, Arg. (a)rāğğāfā 'shake'; passed into ic: Sa. rağāf, Aw. areğefu-ğ`a, Kam. harağfo.

phonetics: `rāğğe` (T.M) = Amh. `ğarāmğa` 'kind of grass resembling a maize plant'
meaning: `kind of grass resembling a maize plant`
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğğe, rağhe, arğah`e 'herbe à touffe vigoureuse pousse des tiges fines, fortes tantes, propres à la confection des paniers' (Beaux-Schreiber 292), Te. arğeh`e 'reed for making baskets'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğhe 'kick, trample on'.

phonetics: `ragama` (yargam) (T) 'curse, insult, execrate, (Y.M) excommunicate'; `taragama`, passive; `tarāğama` 'bind one another by execration'; `ragāmī` 'curser, execrator'; `ragūm` 'cursed, execrated'; `ragmat` 'execration, malediction, curse'; `margam`, same
meaning: `curse, insult, execrate, (Y.M) excommunicate'; `taragama`, passive; `tarāğama` 'bind one another by execration'; `ragāmī` 'curser, execrator'; `ragūm` 'cursed, execrated'; `ragmat` 'execration, malediction, curse'; `margam`, same
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: in view of the various meanings within the development seems to be: 'speak, say against, bring legal action against > curse > cast stones': Ar. rağama 'cast stones, curse', abuse', rāğama 'contend with someone (in words)', Heb. rāğam 'stone, imprecate', Aram. rāğam, Md. rgm, Ug. rgm 'speak, say', Akk. rağāmu 'speak, say'. Noeldeke 1910:47, and Jeffery 140 consider Ar. rağama a Geez loanword, Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907), 620 thinks G. ragama is an Arabic loanword going back to rağīm 'cursed'; ragama would then be a denomina-tive. For more details on this root, see Ahrens 1930:39, Jeffery 140, n.3, Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963):135, Dobberahn 89-90. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāğāmā 'curse', Te. rāğma, Amh.Gur. rāğāmā. Note that Te. rāğma 'stone' is borrowed from Ar. rağama.

(M 139 quoting Tayyā renders it strangely by 'pay, payment'); ETHIOPIC: Amh. rağma 'clotted milk'. Praetorius 1879:183 derives it from Amh. rāğğa (G. rağ`a 'coagulate' with the suffix -m. If this is the case, G. rāğmā would then be an Amharic loanword.

phonetics: `ragana` (yargan) (T.MA.M) 'row, (K*) bind tightly, pull (the sails)'; possibly a denomina-tive from ragn, below
meaning: `row, (K*) bind tightly, pull (the sails)'; possibly a denomina-tive from ragn, below
origin_explanation: ragn, rāgn, (Lt) rāğani, (K*) rāğnāt 'ship, raft, boat, cordage of a ship, sail'. SEMITIC: SAr. rğnt ('Schiffe mit ihren') rğnt Bei-boote oder Takelage' (W. Müller, in an unpublished inscription).
rāğ`nāt 'barley sprout'.

phonetics: `rag`nafa` (T.MA) 'cover the floor with fresh grass or rushes'
meaning: `cover the floor with fresh grass or rushes`
origin_explanation: (MA 207 rag`naka, same meaning, is a misprint for rag`nafa); rag`naf 'fresh grass for spreading'; to be derived from ragafa 1, augmented by n.
SEMITIC: Ar. riğriğa 'muddy remainder of a pool' (so also Armbruster 327). ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. rāğrāğ 'swampy place'.

phonetics: `ragaza` (yargaz) (T) 'pierce, pierce through, transfix, stab, impale'; `taragza`, pass., reflexive; `tarāğaza` 'pierce one another'; `raguz`, (Gr) `rag`az 'pierced'
meaning: `pierce, pierce through, transfix, stab, impale'; `taragza`, pass., reflexive; `tarāğaza` 'pierce one another'; `raguz`, (Gr) `rag`az 'pierced'
origin_explanation: rāğz, pl. rāğzāt (K*) 'doorpost' (the doorpost being conceived of as piercing either the ground or the part of the house to which it is attached); rağzāt (n) 'piercing through, puncture, stab'. SEMITIC: related to Ar. rakaza 'stick or fix in the ground'. ETHIOPIC: Te. rāğza 'pierce', Tna. rāğzā; figura-tively in Amh. (tā)rāğğāzā 'be conceived'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. rağad 'slaughter'. Rei-nisch 1887:289 compares Sa.Af. rahad as against Reinisch 1890:301 where Sa.Af. rahad is compared with Ethiopic hrd 'slaughter'. The Semitic-Ethiopic root is not to be considered a Cushitic loanword as suggested by Cohen 1947:no.165. See also rāğzāt, below.

meaning: anchor (v.), drop anchor, (T.Y) bring to rest, make stop;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Har. (a)râgâza 'settle down, be settled down'

meaning: 'chain'; possibly connected with the root ragaza 'pierce', above.
origin_explanation: possibly connected with the root ragaza 'pierce', above.

is either a misprint for Câd rahṣa from an original Câd rahda (see below), or is to be compared with the root rhd of the various Ethiopian languages (see below). SEMITIC: Mh. rehodî 'shallow water' (W. Müller). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rahda 'be humid, be wet, be soaked', Te. raḥada. Note that Amh. radâ 'become insecure, become spongy (ground)' could be derived from rhd 'be soaked' or from radâ (r'd) 'tremble, shake' (above).
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Te. rahaya 'pond, pool'.

meaning: 'be wide, be large, be broad, be ample, be spacious, be extensive'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. raḥîba 'be wide', SAr. rhb 'enlarge', Soq. raḥab 'large', Heb. rāḥāb 'be wide', rahāb, Ug. rhb. ETHIOPIC: Tna. râḥabâ 'be wide', Te. raḥaba.
gives the impression of being a loanword; possibly from Aram. raḥmânân 'merciful' through Ar. raḥmân (with alternance of labials b:m).

(note that rahadat or raḥdat nafṣu of 4 Kings 6:11 is connected by Dillmann 274 with this root, but it is perhaps to be derived from raḥṣa, raḥṣa 'be disturbed', for which see below) `arḥada` (Gr.143), causative; `raḥud` 'one who perspires, (K*) washed'; `râḥd, (T) râḥṣ` 'sweat, perspiration'; `râḥdat` (Gr.144) 'perspiration'; `marḥad, marḥadî (K*)`, part. of `arḥada`. SEMITIC: Ar. raḥada 'wash', SAr. rhd, Soq. raḥad, Heb. râḥaṣ, Aram. rḥ (Jean-Hoftijzer 278), Ug. rḥṣ, Akk. raḥâṣu 'rinse'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. râḥaṣâ 'perspire', Te. raḥaṣa, râḥaṣa, Gaf. (tâ)raṣâ 'wash', Gur. (tâ)raṭâ. The connection with Amh. râččâ 'spray water' suggested by Amsalu 5 is unlikely since Amh. râččâ goes back to raqaya, below. See also raḥda, above.
SEMITIC: Ar. raḥala 'saddle' (v) > 'mount (a camel), journey' (on the root see Landberg 1923:1182ff), SAr. rḥl 'equipment (e.g. saddle)', Mh. raḥâl 'saddle' (v). See also rahana, below.

meaning: = Amh. wällâqâ 'be taken off, be disjoined'; tāsärra (from śr) 'be designated, be ordained, be imposed, be appointed';
origin_explanation: in view of tarahma (Sw. in D 1394) = Amh. wällâqâ, säffänä, tāsärra, the two roots (name rhm and rhm) seem to go back to one root, and wällâqâ ᎼᎼᎼ is to be corrected into ᎼᎼᎼ.

meaning: 'be finished, be done' (täsärra). However, säffänä 'dominate' does not fit into the meanings of allâqâ, täsärra. See also rhm.

origin_explanation: `arhana` (Gr.143), causative; `tarahna` (Gr.143) 'be loaded, be saddled'; `raḥânî` (Gr.143), act. part. of rahana; `raḥun` (Gr.143) 'loaded, saddled'; `raḥan, with h (T.M)` 'load'; `râḥn (K*)` 'saddle'; `raḥnat` (Gr.143) 'saddling' (n); `marḥan` (Gr.143) 'thong used in fastening loads'; variant of raḥala, above; ETHIOPIC: Tna. raḥanâ 'saddle'; perhaps also `arrânâ` 'load, put a pack on (a pack animal)' m (?) (= laryngeal), with metathesis in relation to rhn.

meaning: 'be far off, be distant some distance (from), go far, keep away, remote, depart, abstain, desist (from), draw, recoil';

origin_explanation: `arḥâqî, marḥaq, marḥaqî` (Gr.142), part. of `arḥaqa`; `mastaraḥaq, part. of `astarahaqa`; `tarahqot` 'apostasy'. SEMITIC: Ar. raḥiq 'far' (dialectal, see Landberg 1923:1181), SAr. rhq 'be distant, be far', Soq. raḥaq, Heb. rāḥaq, Aram.-Syr. raḥaq, Md. rhq, Ug. rhq, Akk. rēqu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. raḥaqâ 'be far', Te. rôḥaqa, Arg. râḥaqa, Har. rôḥaqa, Amh.Gur.Gaf. raqâ.

meaning: 'be humid, be wet, be damp, (Lt) dive, submerge';
origin_explanation: `arḥaṣa` 'wet, water, moisten'; `tarahṣa` (Gr.142) 'be moistened, be watered'; `raḥuṣ (fem. raḥaṣî, raṣṣoḥî)` 'moist, wet, humid, moistened'; `râḥṣ (Lt)` 'humidity'; `raḥṣat` (Gr.142) 'being humid' (n); `arḥâṣî, marḥaṣ (K*)`, part. of `arḥaṣa`; `marḥaṣ (instead of mæhrâṣ of Gr.142)` 'place where one waters or moistens'. SEMITIC: SAr. rhṣ, 'offer libation' (?) (W. Müller 1983:282); possibly related to Ar. raṣṣa 'sprinkle', Syr. raṣ 'besprinkle', Aram. raṣ (rṣṣ) (Dillmann 271), Heb. rṣṣ. ETHIOPIC: Tna. râḥaṣâ 'be humid', Har. rēḥaṣa, Arg. râḥaṣa, Amh. Gur. raṣâ; also Tna. râṣrâṣâ 'soak in water', Amh. râṣrâṣâ 'become wet', from rhṣ > rs > rṣrṣ. Te. râṣrâṣâ 'to water' is borrowed from Ar. raṣṣa. See also raṣha II.

`raḥayî (K*)`, act. participle; `rəḥuy, fem. raḥît` (Gr.141,Lt) 'that which has a good odor, fragrant'; `raḥe, pl. raḥeyât, (Lt) raḥe` 'perfume, flavor, odor, (T) odoriferous plant'; SEMITIC: related to rwh, ryh, as in Ar. rāḥa (rwh) 'smell (a thing)', rīḥa 'smell' (for 'smell' in the various Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1923:1525), Heb. rē'ḥ 'scent' (n), Aram. rēḥâ, Syr. rîḥâ, Md. rîḥa, Ug. rḥ.

meaning: 'be hungry, hunger (for), desire to eat'; also used impersonally (that is, with object suffix pronouns) e.g. yərrabbabanni 'I am hungry'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ragiba 'desire', ragiba 'an 'shun, abstain from' (Brockelmann 1908:227 explains it as a dissimulation process of g > h after r), Heb. rā'eb 'hungry', Ug. rgb. For the root meaning 'desire' and 'be hungry', see Noeldeke 1910:69. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāhabā- 'be hungry', Har. rāhaba-, Amh.Gur. raba-; Tna.Te. rəhab 'hunger', Har. rahab, Arg. rəhaw, Amh. rab. Tigre rəqba 'desire, wish' connected by Cohen 1947:no. 79 is borrowed from Ar. ragiba 'desire'. Reinisch 1887:279 compares it with Bil. na'ab 'be hungry'.

meaning: 'be disturbed, be shaken, be turbulent, be annoyed, become impatient, get angry'; see also rəhda.
'be pampered, be spoiled, be tender'; rəhās, rəhəs, rəhṣ 'tender, delicate, pampered'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. rəhuṣa 'be tender'.

meaning: Amh. H¶ allāqā, H¶ hallāqā (same as H¶) 'be finished' (but perhaps 'be lord', denominative of alāqa), H¶ sūffānā 'dominate', H¶- tāsārra 'be ordained, be appointed, be imposed, be designated'. See also rhm.

tərāhrəha, (T) tərāhrəha 'be compassionate, have compassion, have pity, be moved to pity, have mercy, be gentle, show tenderness'; 'arāhrəha, (L) 'arāhrəha 'appease, soften, soothe, cause to deal graciously, render mild, render kind, induce to tenderness'; rəhruh, (T) rəhruh 'tender, gentle, lenient, soft, merciful, kind'; rəhrəhe 'tenderness, compassion, gentleness, clemency'; 'arāhrəhi (Gr.451), part. of 'arāhrəha; tərāhrəho (t) 'loving kindness, compassion, mercy, pity'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. rəhha 'be soft', rəhrah 'soft'.
probably reconstructed from Amh. rākka 'be satisfied (needs), be sated (from drinking, or a plant from being watered)'.

meaning: 'shoulder blade of an animal which serves as a shovel';
origin_explanation: probably a misprint for rākub I C¶R; see below.

meaning: 'find, get, acquire, obtain, attain, receive, gain, reach, take possession of, possess, overtake, apprehend, invent, find out, discover, perceive, suppose'; with indirect object suffixes, 'come upon, fall upon, befall, occur, come to pass, happen, be becoming to, be proper'; 'arkaba, causative; 'arākaba, passive of rakaba; also 'exist, be available, give an audience'; (zəyərakkāb 'accidental'); (zəyəyərakkāb 'inaccessible, incomprehensible'); tərākaba 'meet one another, hold a meeting, have an interview, go to meet someone, agree with one another, be together, come together, congregate, have intercourse'; with indirect object suffixes, 'come upon, happen, occur'; 'astarkaba ('astarkəba) 'busy oneself eagerly (with), devote oneself (to), apply oneself (to), be at leisure (for), be involved, pay attention, be attentive, be assiduous, be ready, be convenient, be opportune, (T.Y) bring (an offering), (T) unite, harmonize'; 'astarkābā 'cause to meet, cause to come together, bring together, assemble, join, cause to find, be an intermediary, compare'; rakābi, act. part. of rakaba; rakub 'found, contiguous, neighboring, joined that exists, comprehensible'; rakubāt, pl. fem. (Lt) 'beings'; rakubāwi 'pertaining to acquired things'; rakb 'congregation, meeting, council, assembly'; (vəlata rakb 'holiday that is celebrated at mid-Pentecost or on a Wednesday that comes 24 days after Easter'); rakāb 'acquisition'; rukeb (for rukāb) 'joining together'; rukābe 'joining together, intercourse'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Nyberg 235 (following Dillmann 302) connects G. rakaba with Ar. rakiba (see rakaba II) 'sich auf etwas setzen > auf etwas gesetzt, gelegt, plaziert sein > mit etwas nahe, unmittelbar verbun- den sein, mit einem kommen', and he quotes Ar. rakibahu 'he came upon him, he overtook him, he followed closely or immediately after him'. Similarly Ullendorff 1968:128 connects it with Heb. Ar. rkb (hif-il) 'graft, join, connect'. Bittner 1909:117 compares Mh. tarkōb 'transaction, deal' with G. tərākabo(t). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rākābā 'obtain, find', Te. rākba, Har. rākəba, Amh. ('as)rākābā 'hand over', Arg. ('as)rəkkəba, Gur. rākābā 'find, meet'. For G. tərākaba 'have intercourse', cp. Te. tərākəba.
probably reconstructed from markab 'boat'. SEMITIC: Ar. rakiba 'ride, go by boat', SAr. rkb 'ride', Soq. rekob, Heb. rākab, Aram. rakab, raqeb, Syr. rakeb, Md.Ug. rkb, Akk. rakāb.
see also rāk''ə', or could it be a metathesis of Amh. bərakk''a 'a shoulder blade'?

phonetics: rākubāt, rākōbāt, rāk''ōbāt, rākābāt C¶R; C¶R; C¶R; C¶R
origin_explanation: From Ar. rakūb 'camel for riding'; also in Te. rekbay 'pack animal'; in Cushitic: Sa.Af. rakūb. See also 'arkubāt, 'ərkubāt.
From Ar. rikāb; also Te.Tna. rakab, Amh. arkab; in Cushitic: Be.Qem.Sa.Af.Som. rikāb. See also 'ərkāb.

rokum (T.M), rakw (Sw. in D 300) = Amh. sasən 'chest, coffer'; probably comes from an expression such as sāṣəna (sāsəna) rakʿām 'chest of marble' (cp. nəfqa rakʿām 'coffin of marble') and does not mean 'chest, coffer' by itself. from Ar. ruḫām 'marble'; also Amh. rakʿām.

meaning: be unclean, be impure, be polluted, be contaminated, be defiled, be profaned'; (za-iyyəyərakk 'as 'undefiled'); ʾark 'asa 'defile, contaminate, pollute, defame, profane, declare unclean'; tarak 'sa 'be profane, pollute oneself, defile oneself, be declared impure'; tarāk 'asa 'contaminate one another'; ʾastarāk 'asa, (L) ʾastarāk 'sa, caus. of tarāk 'a-sa; also 'pollute, make unclean, abhor, dese-crate, consider impure (unclean, polluted, de-filed)'; rakus (rak's) 'unclean, polluted, impure, de-filed, profane, abominable, corrupt, foul'; rakusāt (pl.fem) 'impurity'; rāk's (raks) 'filth, impurity, defilement, un-cleanness, pollution, abomination, anything un-clean or vile'; rək'sat 'impurity, uncleanness, contamina-tion'; rək'sənnā 'uncleanness'; ʾark 'āsi (Gr.155), part. of ʾark 'asa.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. riks 'dirt', related to riġs, riġs (Am-bros). ETHIOPIC: Tna. rākṣä 'be dirty'. Te. rākṣa, Amh.Gur. rākk 'äsä, Arg. rākkäsa; Te.Tna. rakus 'dirty'. Amh.Arg. ərkus, Gur. ruks; passed into Cush-itic. Kam. ruks 'dirt'. Bil. raġas 'be dirty'. The connection between G. rk's and zalġasa 'be afflicted with sores' < *zargasa < *zarkasa (za-rks) sug-gested by Nyberg 232 (so also in Rundgren 1955: 183) is unlikely.
see fəktam I.

meaning: elevated place, the third heaven, heavenly kingdom'; rāmāwi (Gr.144) 'celestial';
origin_explanation: from Heb. rāmā 'height, high place'; also Amh. rama (from Geez).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. rämaddä 'trample, tread', rämädädä 'trample on heavily', Te. rämädädä 'walk rap-idly without stopping', Tna. rämädädä 'strike, hit'.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps a transferred meaning of Amh. rämädädä 'treat harshly, trample heavily', Tna. rämädädä 'hit'.

meaning: scorch (intr), burn (intr), be burning'; ʾarmada, causative; also 'scorch, con-sume, put in hot ashes'; rəmud (Gr.146) 'burning'; ramad 'hot ashes, heat of ashes'; ramd (K*) 'bread baked in the coals';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ramīda 'be scorched', ramad 'roast on heated stones', Ar. (Dat) ramas, ramad 'be hot', Ṣh. ermid 'put into ashes to cook', Pbh. rāmas 'roll or bake in hot ashes', Aram. rimsā 'hot ashes, embers', Brockelmann 1928:735 compares Syr. rəmūʾā 'hot embers'. On the origin of the month Ramadān, see Landberg 1923:1447. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rämäsä 'heat, cook', Te. rämmaṣa 'be kindled', Amh. rämmaṭä 'bake in a (a)rämmäṭä 'cover a fire with ashes to keep alive'. For G. ramd 'bread baked in the coals', ramṭ. Te. rämda 'suffer from ophthalmia', ramād are borrowed from Ar. ramida and are not to be connected with rmd, as suggested by Reinisch 1887:290.

meaning: spear'; ramha (T.Y.M.Gr.144) 'pierce with a spear' (denominative); ʾarməḥa (Gr.145), causative; taramḥa (Gr.145), passive of ramha; tarāməḥa (Gr.145) 'wound one another with a spear, attack one another with a spear'; ʾastarāməḥa (Gr), caus. of tarāməḥa;
origin_explanation: ramāḥi (Gr.145), act. part. of ramha; ramḥāy (K) 'holder of a spear'; ramḥāwi (Gr.145) 'wounded with a spear'; ramḥat (Gr.145) 'wounding with a spear'. SEMITIC: Ar. ramaha 'pierce with a spear', 'spear', SAr. rmh, Ṣh. rəmah, Heb. romah, Syr. rumḥā. For Ug. mrh, see Yasin 76. ETHIOPIC: Te. rəmḥ 'point of a lance' (possible Ar. rumḥ).
from Ar. raml 'sand', ʾilmu-r-ramli 'geomancy' in Neo-Syr. rimil 'sorcery, divining'; in Cu. Sa.Af. ramal 'geomancy'. Som. ramili; cp. also rml 'sand, omen from geomancy'.

meaning: smooth a hide, rub, polish by rubbing, massage, put a patch on'; M 133 wrongly renders Amh. tafä, the translation of ramma given by Tayyä, 'write', whereas in this context tafä means 'put a patch on'. taramma (K*), passive;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. rämammä 'smooth a hide in order to make parchment'. Amh. rammämma.

meaning: keep silence, be silent, be tranquil, be quiet, remain quiet, rest, make silent, reduce to silence, astound'; taramma 'be passed over in silence'; tarāməma (K*) 'face one another silently'; ʾastarāməma (Gr.451) 'ask to be silent'; tarāməma 'ask to be silent, cause to be silent, reduce to silence, make tranquil'; ramum 'silent, quiet, one who keeps silence'; marmāmi 'silent, quiet, not speaking, who is silent, who makes be silent'; also 'scale, judge' (perhaps originally 'that makes things be quiet or balanced'); ʾərmāmə (Gr.451) 'discipline of keeping silence'; ʾərmāmo (T) 'silence, quiet'; ʾəmbāla ʾərmāmo 'ceaselessly, without intermis-sion'; marmām 'silent'; also participle of ʾarmamma; marmāmi (Gr) 'silent';
origin_explanation: mastarāməmo 'one who obtains silence, who imposes silence'. SEMITIC: Ar. (a)ramma 'be quiet'. There is no relation between this root and Akk. rammamu 'roar', as suggested by von Soden 949. In fact, Akk. rammamu is related to rm (see raʾama, above). ETHIOPIC: Tna. (a)rämmämä 'be silent, be taciturn', Te. rämäm bela, Amh. (a)rämmämä.
the identification of rəmon with ʾaryām, above, sug-gested by Grébeaut 144 on the basis of the quoted text, is doubtful.

SEMITIC: Heb. rimmōn 'pomegranate'. Ar. rummān and G. rəmmān are considered borrowed from Aram.-Syr. rummānā (Fraenkel 142, Noeldeke 1910:42). Ug. ʾirmm-n (Yasin 71), Akk. nurmū, lurmū. On its Hurrite origin, see Dietrich 303. For the bibliography dealing with this noun, see Jeffery 144. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Te.Amh. rəmmān 'pomegranate'.

meaning: remnon 'kind of tree (called dog in Amharic)'; (T quoted by M 139 renders it likewise by Amh. dog 'kind of tree', and not by 're-markable, noble' as given by M 139 who misread Amh. dog for dag 'good'); ʾəda rəmnon 'bramble';
origin_explanation: from Gk. rhamnon, acc. of rhamnos, name of var-ious prickly shrubs; also Amh. ʾəda rəmnon 'a plant' (from Geez).

meaning: be moist-ened, be wet, be pliable, dip dry material in water to dampen it';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (rms), ʾirtamasa 'be dipped into wa-ter'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. rämäsä 'be wet, soak in water and dampen'.

SEMITIC: Ar. ramat 'raft' (for which see Landberg 1923:1437), Soq. remeṣ. Note that Dillmann 276 is probably correct in considering G. rams an Arabic loanword, contrary to Fraenkel 213 and Noeldeke 1910:61 who state that G. rams is common with Ar. ramat.
variant of rabbi, above.
could be a graphic error for ርፕ ፕ, above?

phonetics: rpp, rappa ራፓ ርፓ (MA) = Amh. hararā, translated 'rummage about, look everywhere'
meaning: Amh. hararā, translated 'rummage about, look everywhere'
origin_explanation: note that the Amharic verb is unusual.

phonetics: ropoli ሮፖሊ ርፖሊ (KG), roppolim ሮፖሊም ርፖሊም (K)
origin_explanation: perhaps a corruption of Heb. rōkālīm 'merchants' (Dillmann 1401).
see waraqa.
from the root rqq I be 'thin'; SEMITIC: Ar. raqq 'parchment' is considered a Geez loanword by Fraenkel 246, and Jeffery 133. Syr. raq 'parchment' is borrowed from Arabic.

phonetics: rqq I, raqqa, raqaqa (yəraqraq) ራቃቅ ርቃቅ
origin_explanation: waraqa 'make subtle, soften, make thin'; raqqāq (fem. raqqāq) 'soft, subtle, minute, thin, slender, slight, immaterial'; (ba-raqiqq 'softly'); raqqāq, fem. of raqiq (L) 'small handwriting'; raqq, see above; raqqāt 'softness, subtlety'; SEMITIC: Ar. raqqa 'be thin', Ar. (Dat) raqraqa 'render thin', Heb. raq 'thin', Syr. raqqeq 'make thin'. Ug. rq-m, pl. of rq 'thin cake' (Dietrich, Loretz, OLZ 62[1967].549), Akk. raqāqu 'be thin'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāqāqā 'be thin, be delicate', Amh. rāqqāqā 'be thin, be delicate', Amh. rāqqāqā passed into Cushitic: Aw. arāč-x'a, Kam. rač'avo.
the sequence q-k is unusual. See also sobeqīkā.

phonetics: raqama ራቃማ ርቃማ 'embroider, paint figures on parchment, (T.Y.M) make incisions, write'
meaning: 'embroider, paint figures on parchment, (T.Y.M) make incisions, write'
origin_explanation: (H 222 wrongly renders Amh. salā [translation of raqama] by 'be sharp' instead of 'paint design'); raqāqa (Gr.149), causative; taraqama (Gr.149), pass. of raqama; raqāmi (Gr.149), act. part. of raqama; raqum (Gr.149) 'embroidered'; raqmat (Gr.149) 'act of embroidering'; SEMITIC: Ar. raqama 'embroider', Heb. rāqam 'weave in color', Aram. riqmətā 'checks, spots', Syr. raq'tā 'freckles'. See on this root Rabin 1970:4ff. ETHIOPIC: in the meaning 'write', G. raqama is borrowed from Ar. raqama.

meaning: 'sprinkle, perse, sprinkle with holy water to drive out demons, cleanse with holy water, (Lt) purify, exorcise, recite incantations, charm, use conjurer's trick'
origin_explanation: urqaya (K*), causative; taraqaya, pass. of raqaya; also 'be fixed (spell)'; raqāyi, raqāyy (pl. raqayāt), act. part. of raqaya; also 'sorcerer, charmer, exorcist'; raqāy, same; raqay (fem. raqīt) 'exorcised, bewitched, enchanted, (K.Gr.150) sprinkled'; raqāy 'charm, incantation'; raqayāt, raqayāt 'sprinkling, incantation, exorcism, enchantment, charm'; marqāy 'place of exorcism'; marqay (Gr.150) 'sprinkler for holy water'; Noeldeke 1910:63 wrongly considers the possibility of two roots: rqy I 'sprinkle', and rqy II 'exorcise'. As for Amh. and Tna. mārrāqā given by Noeldeke, ib., it means 'spit, bless' (see Leslau 1979:422) and is not to be connected with raqaya. SEMITIC: Ar. raqā (rqy) 'enchant', Sar. rq-t 'female magician'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāqāyā 'sprinkle, sprinkle holy water (on a place or on a person)', Amh. rāččā 'sprinkle water' (with qy > č); see Cohen 1931:398, Leslau 1979:XXXIX (5.41), LX (9.33). Arg. rāčča, Gur. rečča passed into Cushitic: Aw. arāč-x'a, Kam. rač'avo.

meaning: 'seek by feeling for (something), wish to discover by feeling, grope, (T) trample, flatten (troop of soldiers: vegetables)'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)rāmsmāsa 'rummage', Amh. ərməsmass 'all walk gropingly, feel around like a blind person'. See also ramsamsa.

aramsamsa, (T.Y) 'aramsamsa 'grope, feel around groping, search in many places, (T.Y) bustle about, trample, flatten'; metathesis of marsasa, above; SEMITIC: the root is not to be connected with Syr. ramisā 'sedate, moderate' as suggested by Brockelmann 1928:735. Koehler 895 connects the Geez root with Heb. rāmas 'creep'; Ar. ramas'a 'take with the fingers' is less likely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ('a)rāmsāmsā 'grope', Te. ('a)rāmsāsa. See also rmsms.

meaning: 'throw, pierce, strike by throwing, strike with a spear, (Gr.146) = Amh. attārā 'scar, form (scar having small, pea-like protuberances)'
origin_explanation: armaya (Gr.146) 'make scarred, scar (tr)'; taramya, taramaya, pass. of ramaya, (Gr.146) 'be scarred'; ramāyi (K*), act. part. of ramaya; rəmuy 'pierced'; rəmyat, ramet 'piercing, wound, injury, infected wound, ulcer, swelling of a wound, skin rash, (Gr.146) scar'; SEMITIC: Ar. ramā (rmy) 'throw, shoot', Heb. rāmā (rmy), Aram.-Syr. ramā (rmy), Md. rma, Ug. rmy (de Moor 313), Akk. ramū.
from Gk. rhōmaisti 'in Latin'.

meaning: 'puncture, cut a vein in the forehead in order to bleed or cup a person'
origin_explanation: raqāba (T) 'butcher' (it has the form of a plural); SEMITIC: Tna. rāqhe 'cut by puncturing a vein in the forehead', perhaps also Amh. rāqərrāqā 'beat or strike repeatedly' (from rq? > rq > rqrq). See also marqəha.

phonetics: raqaha II ራቃሃ ርቃሃ (MA 33) = Amh. rāččā 'sprinkle, spray' (note the misprint in MA 'répondre' instead of 'répandre')
origin_explanation: (note the misprint in MA 'répondre' instead of 'répandre'); ETHIOPIC: possibly related to raqaya I.
denominative from raqq 'leaf of paper'.

phonetics: raqet ራቄት ርቄት, raqit ራቂት ርቂት

origin_explanation: see also ridon II.
from Amh. ras.

phonetics: ros ሮስ ርስ, rosa ሮሳ ርሳ
rasa (yərsā) ራሳ ርሳ, (T) ras'a ራሳ ርሳ, (Y) ras'a ራሳ ርሳ
meaning: 'forget, overlook, be negligent, neglect, err, act impiously, become godless, be wicked'
origin_explanation: (the meaning 'become old' given by Gr.148 belongs to ras'a); ars'a, caus. of ras'a; also 'induce to error'; taras'a, passive; 'also 'fall into error, fall into impiety, escape one's notice, be ignorant'; ras'ā, act. part. of ras'a.

phonetics: rāsa 'forgetful, negligent, impious, ungodly'; rasu 'forgotten, impious' (the meaning of Amh. yaraggā 'who became old' given by Tayyā belongs to rašā)
meaning: rasi 'godless, ungodly, impious, wicked, wrongful, sinful'; ras 'forgetfulness, godlessness, ungodliness'; ras'at 'forgetfulness, negligence, impiety'; ras'ān, ras'annat, same. marsa, marsa'i 'that which makes forgetful'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. rasa'a 'be slack', Heb. rāša' 'err willfully', Aram.-Syr. raša' 'be wicked', Akk. ruššû 'treat ruthlessly' (for which see von Soden 996). For a connection between 'forget' and 'err', cp. Heb. tā'ā (ry); see E.Y. Kutscher, Leshonenu 31 (1967), 109-10. Note, however, that Schwally, ZDMG 52 (1898), 135 considers G. rs' in the meaning 'act impiously' as being borrowed from Syriac. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāssa'e 'forget', Te. (tə)rāssa'a, Har. rāsa'a, Amh. rāssa, Gur. rāsa.

meaning: 'render impure, make turbid, convict of crime, accuse, condemn, prove guilty, reproach, suspect, rebuke, blame, confound, chastise, convince';
origin_explanation: from rsh (see rasha I) with reduplication of the last two radicals.

meaning: 'glow, be red-hot, be heated, be inflamed'; 'arsana 'make red-hot, heat, burn, ignite, try or test (a metal, with fire)'; rasun 'red-hot, glowing, heated, fiery, tried (a metal, with fire), ripe'; rasn (rasn) 'heat, burning heat, heating, incandescence'; (mā)ətaba rasn 'brand mark'; rasnat (n) 'heating, burning, inflammation, cauterizing, ardor'; 'arsāni, marsan, part. of 'arsana.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) rasin 'burn' (for which see Landberg 1923:1281, and Praetorius, ZDMG 57 [1903], 273). As for Ar. rasin, Praetorius, ib. is inclined to consider it a Persian (rošan) loanword, as against Landberg, ib. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāsänä 'be very hot', Te. räsna 'glow', Amh. rāssänä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. rasän.
From Ar. raṣāṣ 'bullets, lead'; also Amh.Gur. arsas, rasas, Te. rasas, raṣāṣ, Tna. rasas, raṣāṣ, arṣaṣ, raṣāṣ; in Cushitic: Sa. riṣāṣ, Af. ruṣāṣ, Bil. raṣāṣ.
see warasa.

meaning: 'be unclean, be dirty, be filthy, be polluted, be impure, be defiled, be contaminated, (Lṭ) be dull, be lusterless, rust, be liable (to), be subject (to), be doomed, be accused, be guilty'; 'aršaha, causative; also 'defile, stain, pollute, contaminate, condemn, accuse'; rasāhi (KG), act. part. of rasha; rasuh 'defiled, dirty, filthy, impure, accused, charged (with), guilty, (Gr) dull, lusterless'; rashā (L) 'accused of crime'; rash (Gr.147), rash, rashā (K*) 'impurity, dirt'; rashat 'defilement, impurity, pollution, dirt, uncleanness, filth, crime, guilt, accusation'; 'aršāhi (Lṭ), part. of 'aršaha; 'aršahšaha, see rshsh.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: F. Perles 1917:304 connects it with PbH. širaḥ 'decay', Aram. šaraḥ (with metathesis). The identification with Heb.Aram.Syr. rṣṣ 'sprinkle', Ar. rašša, Akk. ruššû, "etwa '(durch Wasser) aufweichen'" suggested by von Soden 996 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāshe 'be dirty', Te. rāsha. See also rašha.
metathesis of rahsa, above.

meaning: 'put, place, set, set up, establish, institute, constitute, do, make, convert (into), arrange, treat, bestow, appoint, prepare, esteem, consider, declare, impute, regard thing as, clothe, arm, equip, (Gr.148) decorate, adorn'; (rassaya ra'aso 'he valued himself, he esteemed himself');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. rs'y 'prepare for' (Biella 490, but not SabDic); perhaps Syr. tariṭā 'sustenance, supplies' (from a root rsy), as suggested by Brockelmann 1928:836. ETHIOPIC: Gur. (a)rāšä (from rsy) 'build (a house), weave (clothes), plait (the hair)'; perhaps also Gf. riššä 'share'.

meaning: 'drop anchor, bring to rest'; marsay (Lṭ) 'refuge'; marso, (T) mars 'port, harbor, anchor, haven, (T) quiet, tranquility, (Sw. in D 280) quiet place';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. raṣā (rsy) 'lie at anchor', 'arsā 'drop anchor', marsā 'port, anchorage' (Noeldeke 1910:61 does not consider Ar. marsā a Geez loanword as stated in Jeffery 262), Soq. riši 'be fixed, be attached', Hars. reṣā 'stand firm', 'arṣā 'anchor' (v). This root is not to be connected with Syr. tarṣi (denominative from tarṣitā, from rsy) 'support, supply (with)', as suggested by Brockelmann 1928:836. For the Syriac root, see rassaya. Note that barrūṣi 'anchor', used in the Arabic dialects of South Arabia as well as in Modern South Arabian, derives from an Indian language and is not to be identified with marso as suggested by various authors mentioned in Leslau 1938:96, D. Cohen 1976:86. For the India origin of barrūṣi, see Landberg 1920:153-4, and Leslau, ib. ETHIOPIC: Te. rāṣa (rsy) 'drop anchor', mārṣo 'harbor', Tna. mārṣo, Amh. mārs, mārṣo 'port, harbor' (from Geez). See also rsw, mrss II, marsawa.

meaning: 'become aged, grow old (occasionally of things)'; ras'a, causative; taras'a (Lṭ) 'become aged together'; ras'w (Lṭ), rasu' (T), rasu' (Lṭ) 'old, old man, advanced in age'; ras' 'old age, age'; ras'at 'getting old (n), old age'; ras'ān 'old age'; ras'annā 'old age'; marsa', marsa'i (K*), part. of 'arsā'a.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ph. of Karatepe rš 'be old' (G. Goldenberg, in Tur-Sinai, Halashon wahasefer III [1955], p.410-11). ETHIOPIC: Amh. rāssa 'become old, age' (KBT, Amharicized from G. ras'a).

meaning: 'become callused (foot), swell (foot)'; probably to be identified with rasha I.
origin_explanation: probably to be identified with rasha I.
see rws.
SEMITIC: Ar. ruḍāb 'sucked saliva'.

meaning: = Amh. man tamar 'how beautiful she is!';
origin_explanation: probably an interpretation of a personal name (Getatchew). In fact, on the line below (in T 387) there is ruṣfā, proper name of a woman, which may be a misprint for ruṣāfā.
see rws.
see rwṭ.

meaning: 'be (become) straight, be straightened, be right, be righteous, be just, be truthful, be sincere, become established, be restored, be well-ordered, be successful, prosper, be proper; also used with indirect object suffix pronouns; e.g. raṭ'anni 'it seemed right to me'; 'arṭa'a 'straighten, make right, render straight, set up, stabilize, redress, justify, rectify, convince, correct, establish, direct, succeed, declare right or true, declare in someone's favor'; ('arṭa'a fənoṭo 'he went toward');

origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Sar. rṭṭ 'dispose, settle', rṭṭr-m 'directive', perhaps also Soq. (1) rṭṭ (with metathesis) 'do something well', Akk. reṭū 'strengthen'. The root is not to be identified with Syr. reṭyā 'warning, admonition', PbH. rāṭu:y 'proper', as suggested by Noeldeke 1910:98, n. 1, Brockelmann 1928:746. Ar. ratā:a 'have their fill (cattle), enjoy a luxurious life (man)' suggested by Amsalu 16 (so also by Rabin 1969:151) is a secondary formation of 'irṭa:a, from rṭy 'graze, pasture'. The connection with PbH. šarṭu:a 'spear' (from ša-rṭu:a 'what is straight'), suggested by Nyberg 198, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāṭe 'win', Te. rāṭa 'be right, be straight, win a lawsuit', Har. rāṭa:a 'win a lawsuit', Amh.Arg. rāṭa, Gur. rāṭa: passed into Cushitic: Dar. raṭṭa:e.

meaning: 'set right, keep straight, regulate, set in order, establish, improve, reform, restore, uphold'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Sar. rṭṭ 'dispose, settle', rṭṭr-m 'directive', perhaps also Soq. (1) rṭṭ (with metathesis) 'do something well', Akk. reṭū 'strengthen'. meaning: 'upright, straight, just, right, righteous, honest, true, correct, proper, level, even, orthodox'; followed by the subjunctive it means 'it is seemly (proper, appropriate)'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Sar. rṭṭ 'dispose, settle', rṭṭr-m 'directive', perhaps also Soq. (1) rṭṭ (with metathesis) 'do something well', Akk. reṭū 'strengthen'. meaning: 'uprightness, rectitude, righteousness, justice, integrity, equity (in judgment), honesty, truth, truthfulness'

meaning: pl. mastarātā:āt (Gr.152) 'one who corrects, who straightens out, soldier of the guard, executioner'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Sar. rṭṭ 'dispose, settle', rṭṭr-m 'directive', perhaps also Soq. (1) rṭṭ (with metathesis) 'do something well', Akk. reṭū 'strengthen'. meaning: 'be humid, be damp, be wet, be moist, be sappy, be juicy, be fresh, be green (tree)'
origin_explanation: urtāṭa, causative; also 'grow green, make green (a tree)'; raṭub 'damp, wet, moist, humid, fresh, green (tree), damp place'; raṭb 'sap'; raṭbat 'dampness, humidity, freshness (of tree)'; raṭbannā (L.Gr.160), same; SEMITIC: Ar. raṭuba 'be wet', Heb. raṭab, Syr. raṭeb, Md. rṭb, Akk. raṭābu. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. rāṭabā 'be wet', Amh.Arg. rāṭṭaba, Har. ruṭṭa 'wet' (from rṭb, with b > zero).
raṭana (T.Y.M) 'cure with balm' (denominative) from Gk. rhētínē 'resin, gum'; also Syr. reṭīnā 'resin', Ar. raṭīna:g, raṭīn 'pine resin', latr II.
for *raṭal, variant of latr, latr, above. For raṭl, cp. also Ar. raṭl, Aram.-Syr. riṭlā. ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh. rāṭal 'weight of 30 ounces', Amh. also nāṭr.

meaning: = Amh. čāqāččāqā, could be either 'importune, pester', or 'become muddy'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: perhaps Ar. rāba (rwb) 'be perplexed'.

meaning: 'pursue, go after, persecute, rush upon, press upon, capture by assault, attack, assail, make an incursion, invade, taroda, tarawda, passive; ra:wdi act. part. of roda; also 'enemy' ra:wwud, ra:wwud, pass. part. of roda; ra:wid (L), ra:widot (Lt) 'persecution'; rudat, verbal noun of roda; also 'persecution', 'incursion' tarawādi 'persecuted, captured by assault'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. rāda (rwd) 'prowl, search for food', Heb. rād (rwd) 'roam', related to Soq. ro:di (rdy) 'steal'. The connection with Akk. rādu 'tremble' suggested by Koehler 876 (under rwd), von Soden 941, and Conti 1978:32, n. 2 is unlikely since its root is r:d, for which see ra:da, above.

meaning: 'fan, make a breeze by fanning. (T.Y.M) wave a fly whisk, scatter, sprinkle'; taro:ha (K*), passive; 'arāwəha (T) 'use a fan'; tarāwəha 'flap the wings making a breeze, rise (wind), fan (a dignitary) together or in turn'; marāwəh (K*), marāwəht, marāwəht, marāwəh (D.L), marāwəht (Lt., with metathesis), pl. marāwəhtāt, marāwəhtāt, marāwəhtāt, marāwəhtāt; wāht 'fan, sprinkler, (Sw. in D 306) fly whisk'; marwāh (K*) 'sprinkler, (T) fan' (from Ar. mirwāh);
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. rāha (rwh) 'blow upon (wind)', 'arwāha 'fan oneself', mirwāh 'fan' (for this root in the various Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1923:1524), Šh. rwh 'fan' (v), marbaht/moruh 'fan' (n), Heb. rū:h 'wind', Aram. rūḥā, Syr. rūḥ 'breath', marūḥā 'fan' (n), Md. ruh 'blow', Ug. rḥ 'wind'. ETHIOPIC: Te. rawāha 'fan' (v), mārāwəht 'fan' (n), Amh. mārwa (Ludolf) 'fan' (from Geez); also in Cushitic: Sa. rōh 'blow', rōhe 'breath, soul', So. marawāhad 'fan', from Ar. mirwaha. See also (wrh) marawāht.

meaning: 'clank' (v); marāwəht, marāwəht, marāwəht, pl. marāwəhtāt, marāwəhtāt, marāwəhtāt, marāwəh (L.Gr.85) marāwəht 'church bell'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: it is quite possible that roha in the meaning 'clank' is reconstructed by K on the basis of the noun marawəh 'bell'. As for 'bell', cp. Tna. mārāwəh, marāwəhta 'church bell', Amh. mārāwəha, mārāwət. W. Müller, ZDMG 131 (1981),401 suggests a connection between marawəht 'fan' and marawəht 'bell' from a root rwh on the basis of Chr.Ar. mirwāh 'eine fächerartige Metallscheibe mit Cherubdarstellungen zum Gebrauch bei der Messe', as given by Graf 54. See also marāwəht.
it is strange that it has the same meaning as rawala which occurs in Y 331 on the line above.
SEMETIC: Ar. rawh 'rest, quiet, happiness, gladness, mercy'.
see rwh III.
SEMETIC: Grimme 1914:264 identifies it with Semitic rbb 'have authority over': Ar. rabba, and others.
from Ar. falāsifa-r-ruwāq (name given to the Stoics who met in a portico) from Ar. ruwāq, riwāq 'portico'; also Te. rəwāq. For the concept of Ar. riwāq, cp. Gk. stoa 'portico', hence 'Stoic'.

meaning: 'give birth to, engender'; ros, (T.M) rosā 'male, child'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Gur. abāros, abāros 'household, family, servants and slaves of the family' from ab(ā) ros 'owner of ros'. Note that ros, rus means 'slave' in Gurage, but 'male' in Oromo.

meaning: 'run about'; 'arosa, 'arasa 'cause to run'; tarāwəsa 'run together, run as a group, flock together, compete in running, vie with'; (gānen yəṭərawwəsā 'the demon possesses her'); 'astarāwəsa (Gr.157) 'make run by intermixing'; ra:wsi 'runner, swift, prompt, assiduous'; ra:wwəs, ra:wwəs 'who runs, running'; (ra:wwəsa 'speedily'); ra:wsi 'race'; ru:ṣat 'running, run, track, pathway, course, contest'; 'arwāṣo 'afrās 'horse races'; marwāṣi 'who causes horses to run'; marwāṣ 'course, track, race, contest, arena, distance run, orbit';
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Heb. rāṣ (rwṣ), Aram.-Syr. rəheṭ, Md. rḥi, Akk. rāṣu. For Arabic, Barth 1902:45 quotes riyāda 'movement', as against sukūn 'quiet'. For Ug. rẓ, see Caquot 257. See on this root Schulthess 68-9.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. rāwāt, rawāt ‘a kind of hood that hangs down in back’

meaning: ‘rub, rub off, rub down, scrape, polish’
origin_explanation: rawāṭi (KG), act. participle; ETHIOPIC: Tna. ratāwā (with metathesis) ‘rub, scrape’.

meaning: ‘drink one’s fill, be satisfied with drink, be watered’
origin_explanation: ‘arwaya ‘give to drink, make drink, quench thirst, saturate, inebriate, water, irrigate’; taraw(w)aya, tarawwaya ‘be satisfied with drink, be inebriated, be irrigated’; rawāyi (K*), act. part. of rawaya; rawwuy, rawwəy (fem. rawwəyī), rawwīṭ) ‘satiated with drink, watered, irrigated’; rāwyaṭ ‘drinking one’s fill, providing with drink’; marway (T.K) ‘leather bottle, canteen, tumbler’; marwāy (Gr) ‘place of refreshment, place where one is satisfied with drink’; SEMITIC: Ar. rawiya ‘drink one’s fill’, Sar. h-rwy ‘provide a water supply’, Soq. re (rwy) ‘drink’, Heb. rāwā ‘drink one’s fill’, Aram.Syr. rawi, Md. rwa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. rāwāyā ‘be satiated with drink’, Te. rāwa.
perhaps a misprint in relation to ርዘ foza, above.

perhaps rather rayā; from Ar. rāya through Amh. raye ‘flag’. Note that Sw. in D 1401 has rayā = Amh. alag, a word that is not recorded in any Amharic dictionary; it is probably a misprint for alama as given in Tayyā.
(T ርዳ rayyasa = Amh. ርዳ-bārra ‘be bright’ is a misprint for ርዳ gārra ‘tame’); ‘arayyada (K*), causative;

tarāyada (Gr.158) ‘exercise’ (intr)’ ‘astarāyada ‘tame, train’; rayyādi ‘tamer, trainer’; rayyud ‘tamed, trained, broken in’ rayyāde (K*) ‘taming’ (n); SEMITIC: Ar. rāda (rwd) ‘tame, train, domesticate’, Heb. rwş ‘break a horse’ (see Barr 145), Syr. marū-şānā (from rwş) ‘horse breaker’. For Ug. rz, see Caquot 257.

rayyum (Gr.157) ‘raised, elevated’; rayyām (Lt) ‘high, elevated’; ‘aryām, see above; SEMITIC: Ar. rayyama ‘exceed’, raym ‘superiority, surplus’ (for rym in the Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1923:1638ff), Sar. rym-m ‘height’, Heb. rām (rwm, rym) ‘be high’, Aram.-Syr. rām, Md. rum, Ug. rw/y/m. See also rāmā.
from Ar. ra’īs ‘director, superintendent’. See also ra’sa.

meaning: ‘large white bird which feeds on locusts’
origin_explanation: according to R.H. Charles, The Book of Jubilees (1902), p. 219, n. 23, rāzā is wrongly rendered ‘rice’ by Dillmann 312 instead of ‘white bird which feeds on locusts’; ETHIOPIC: Tna.Te.Amh. raza ‘kind of big bird’; also Cushitic: Qem. rāzā. See also ‘arāzā.

from Ar. ruzz < ‘aruzz (of Tamil [arici] origin; Iranian viriŋ, biring); also in the other Ethiopian languages. As for Geez ruzā, it is either from Syr. ruzā or from Gk. oryza. For ruzz, see also Landberg 1923:1242-3, Rabin, JSS 11 (1966), 5, Rabin 1968:436. For ‘rice’, see I. Löw, ZA 21 (1908), 206ff.

meaning: ‘be dazed, be dizzy, turn (head, intr), be drunk’
origin_explanation: razāmi (K*), act. participle; razum (K*) ‘dazed, dizzy’; marzam, marzām ‘perplexity, dizziness, (T) that which makes the head turn or causes dizziness, (K*) turning of the head’; ETHIOPIC: Te. märzām ‘headache’.

meaning: ‘become long, be tall, be heavy, become proud’
origin_explanation: ‘arzama (K*), causative;
razāmi (K*), act. participle; seems to render Heb. rāzam ‘wink’; it corresponds to Ar. ramaza (with metathesis), PbH.Aram.Syr.Md. rmz.

SEMITIC. onomatopoetic: Ar. qaˀqaˀ, qawqaˀa 'make noise', qaˀqaˀ 'crow', Heb. qaˀa (qˀy) 'cry out', PbH. qiqaˀ, Syr. qˀaˀ 'cry out, shout', qˀaˀa 'crow', Neo-Syr. qwaqaˀ 'raven'; related to Heb. gaˀa (gˀy) 'shout', Syr. gˀa. ETHIOPIC. Te. qawa 'crow', qaˀ 'bela 'croak aloud, make a noise', qoq 'bela 'croak', Tna. qaˀ 'bälä, qˀaˀ 'äbbälä 'make a noise', Amh. qˀaˀ 'alä 'make a noise', qäwwa 'to crow', an-qaqqa 'croak', Gur. qˀaˀ 'epä 'produce a sound', qawqaw 'bälä 'make a noise and wake up others'; also in Cushitic: Som. qaw, qawqaw, interjection expressing noise, Bil. qäq 'shout', Be. kˀikˀäy 'raven', Af. käkäö, Mo. qäkko. The root is related to Amh. kˀaˀa 'alä 'make a noise', Tna. kˀah 'bälä (see Leslau 1979:331, and kaka, ib.340). See also guga I, and kˀäkˀä 'a raven'.
Note that Amh. qʷaˀa 'moon' (translation of qʷaˀä) of T 156 is a misprint for qʷaˀa 'unripe'. See also qoˀ.

meaning: 'blossom of grapevine, petal, bud, unripe fruit, sour grape, acidity, immature age'
origin_explanation: qoˀa (Y.M), qoˀa (T) 'be unripe, be sour (grape), pick unripe fruit' (denominative); ETHIOPIC. Amh. qoˀ 'unripe fruit'; also in Cushitic: Som. qoy 'unripe fruit'. See also qʷaˀä II, and qawä.
qangädaawa 'look out, lift up (the eyes), look up with devotion, be devout'; qangädaaw 'one who looks upward (with devo-tion), devout';

meaning: 'be fat'; maqʷˀäl, maqʷˀal (pl. maqʷˀäl, maqʷˀälit) 'fat, fat animal, fat ox, an animal fattened for sacrifice'; (maqʷˀäla lähm [Lt] 'suckling calves'); (maqʷˀäla tali [Lt] 'fattened kids');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC. Syr. qˀad 'bend' suggested by Dillmann 456 (also Stade 44) is not to be identified with the Geez root since only a concept 'bend (the head) backward' could explain the meaning of lifting up the eyes, of looking upward. it is possible that K reconstructed the verb qʷaˀala, or it could be a denominative from maqʷˀäl which is to be compared with Be. mikʷˀal 'marrow'. Reinisch 1895:167 compares the Be. noun with Ar. mukäl 'fat'. ETHIOPIC: Te. maqˀal 'marrow' (not maqʷˀal as in Reinisch 1895:167).

meaning: 'press together, squeeze together, (Gr.421) load (an animal)'; taqaˀana (T.K), taqaˀana (Y) 'be pressed to-gether (crowd), be afflicted, be grieved'; maqˀaˀan, (Lt) maqˀaˀan 'pressed together, squeezed, tightened, compressed, narrow, hard, austere'; maqˀan, (K) maqˀan 'narrow path, narrow place, instrument serving for inflicting torture';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Amh. mäqan 'doorframe, doorpost' (that is, 'a narrow passage'). See also maqana, maqna.
from Amh. qaba; also in Te. qäba 'a disease', Tna. qaba; passed into Cushitic: Bil. qäba 'illness'. See also qabawa.
